questions.text: GIRES - "Gestion intégrée des ressources" is a software development failure story because of:
questions.explanation: GIRES is a classic case study of software project failure in Quebec. It suffered from extreme budget overruns (project cost), constant scope creep (requirements changes), and technical instability (bad software quality).

questions.text: __________ ensure that project stakeholders work for the same reasons and get agreement on requirements.
questions.explanation: Business requirements establish high-level goals and objectives. They provide the strategic context that ensures all stakeholders are aligned on the purpose and 'why' of the project.

questions.text: Requirements that do not help achieve a business objective should be:
questions.explanation: To maintain project scope and avoid unnecessary costs, any requirement that does not directly contribute to a defined business objective should be removed (eliminated).

questions.text: __________ determine what factors contribute to the problem that may not be apparent.
questions.explanation: Root cause analysis is a methodology used to dig deeper into problems to find their underlying origins rather than just addressing visible symptoms.

questions.text: Goals of software requirements elicitation include determination of:
questions.explanation: Requirement elicitation involves identifying where to get information (sources), how to get it (techniques), and defining what is included in the project (scope).

questions.text: Software engineers apply a percentage of __________ on applying techniques, methods, and standards during a software development project.
questions.explanation: In many software engineering management models, it is estimated that only about 20% of effort is spent directly on applying formal technical methods and standards, while the majority is spent on communication and management.

questions.text: __________ can play the role of customer if the software is developed for the general public.
questions.explanation: When there is no specific client (e.g., commercial off-the-shelf software), marketing specialists act as proxies for the general public to identify needs and define requirements.

questions.text: During requirements elicitation, a software engineer does all the following, EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Negotiation is typically considered a distinct phase that happens after elicitation, where conflicts discovered during the gathering and analysis of requirements are resolved.

questions.text: Software requirements elicitation activities are conducted:
questions.explanation: Modern requirements elicitation is both iterative (repeating activities to gain depth) and incremental (adding more details over time).

questions.text: The product ________ defines the high-level goals for the kind of product such that it may affect multiple projects or releases.
questions.explanation: A product Vision document describes the long-term purpose and context of the product, which can guide multiple related projects.

questions.text: Mean time to failure (MTTF) is a measure of software:
questions.explanation: Reliability is defined as the probability of failure-free operation for a specified time. MTTF is a standard metric used to quantify this.

questions.text: The percentage of target dependent statements in a codebase is an example of a ________ measurement.
questions.explanation: Portability refers to how easily software can be moved between environments. High target-dependency reduces portability.

questions.text: Business rules are a source of software ________ requirements.
questions.explanation: Business rules define both what the system must do (functional) and the constraints or policies it must follow (non-functional).

questions.text: According to NIST, approximately what percentage of software defects are introduced during the specification phase?
questions.explanation: Studies from NIST and other organizations show that around 70% of software defects originate in the requirements and specification phases.

questions.text: ________ provide constraints on how the system should be designed and built (e.g., specific programming languages or standards).
questions.explanation: Process requirements constrain the development process itself, such as requiring a specific methodology or coding standard.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the standard activities in the requirements engineering process?
questions.explanation: The requirements engineering process includes Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation, and Management. Compilation is part of the implementation phase.

questions.text: Software engineers often face problems during elicitation. Which category of problem involves stakeholders being unsure of what they want or expressing requirements in their own terms?
questions.explanation: Problems of understanding occur when there are communication gaps between stakeholders and engineers, or when stakeholders have trouble articulating their needs.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between user requirements and system requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: User requirements are for developers; system requirements are for users.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User requirements are abstract statements in natural language; system requirements are detailed technical descriptions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: There is no difference between them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: System requirements define hardware; user requirements define software.

questions.explanation: User requirements are high-level and written for the customer, while system requirements provide a detailed specification for the development team.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a brainstorming session during requirements elicitation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To generate a high volume of diverse ideas in a non-critical environment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To finalize the system's database schema.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To perform formal validation of existing requirements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To write the project's test cases.

questions.explanation: Brainstorming is a creative technique used to gather as many ideas and requirements as possible from a group of stakeholders.

questions.text: In requirements engineering, what is the value of using 'scenarios'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They provide a detailed mathematical proof of correctness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They provide real-world examples of how a system is used to perform a specific task.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They replace the need for a final requirements document.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are used solely for calculating project costs.

questions.explanation: Scenarios help stakeholders visualize system behavior by walking through real-life examples of interactions between users and the system.

questions.text: Which elicitation technique involves a facilitator leading a group of stakeholders through an intensive, focused session to reach consensus on requirements?
questions.explanation: Facilitated workshops (like Joint Application Development) bring different stakeholders together to resolve conflicts and define requirements collectively.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a significant risk during the requirements elicitation process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stakeholders having conflicting views on system priorities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Requirements that change frequently over time (volatility).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implicit requirements that stakeholders assume are 'obvious' and fail to mention.

questions.explanation: Conflicting views, volatility, and tacit knowledge are all major challenges that can lead to incomplete or incorrect requirements.

questions.text: In the Dilbert comic provided, the user states she won't know what she can accomplish until the engineer tells her what the software can do. This represents which common elicitation problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The 'I'll know it when I see it' (IKIWISI) syndrome

questions.explanation: The comic illustrates the IKIWISI syndrome, where users find it difficult to define requirements until they have a concrete system or prototype to interact with.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly identifies the six standard phases of the Requirements Engineering (RE) process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inception, Elicitation, Analysis & Negotiation, Specification, Validation, and Management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inception, Development, Coding, Specification, Deployment, and Management

choices.label: C
choices.text: Planning, Analysis, Design, Implementation, Testing, and Maintenance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation, Planning, Execution, Monitoring, Validation, and Closing

questions.explanation: The Requirements Engineering process typically includes: Inception (starting the process), Elicitation (discovering requirements), Analysis & Negotiation (resolving conflicts), Specification (documenting), Validation (checking consistency), and Management (handling evolution).

questions.text: Challenges encountered during requirements elicitation are generally categorized into which three main types of problems?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scope problems, Understanding problems, and Volatility problems

choices.label: B
choices.text: Functional problems, Technical problems, and Financial problems

choices.label: C
choices.text: Design problems, Coding problems, and Debugging problems

choices.label: D
choices.text: Legal problems, Ethical problems, and Social problems

questions.explanation: Requirements elicitation challenges typically fall into three categories: Scope (boundaries), Understanding (interpretations), and Volatility (changing needs or lack of commitment).

questions.text: In software engineering, how are 'User requirements' distinguished from 'System requirements'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: User requirements are goals stakeholders expect the system to achieve, whereas system requirements are requirements for the system to be built as a whole.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User requirements are technical specifications, while system requirements are informal goals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: User requirements describe hardware limits, while system requirements describe software logic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User requirements are created by developers, while system requirements are created by project managers.

questions.explanation: User requirements focus on stakeholder goals and functions, while system requirements provide a holistic view of the technical requirements for the system's construction.

questions.text: During the Requirements Engineering process, which phase involves checking the requirements document specifically for consistency and completeness?
questions.explanation: Requirements Validation is the check performed on the requirements document to ensure it is accurate, complete, and consistent with stakeholder needs.

questions.text: In a brainstorming session for requirements, what is the primary goal of the activity known as 'The Calm'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Filtering, prioritizing, and clarifying generated ideas to keep the best ones.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generating as many ideas as possible regardless of their quality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assigning roles like scribe and moderator to the participants.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stopping all discussion to allow participants to take a rest.

questions.explanation: Brainstorming consists of 'The Storm' (quantity-focused generation) and 'The Calm' (quality-focused filtering and prioritization).

questions.text: What is an example of a 'problem of volatility' that can occur during requirements elicitation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stakeholders will not commit to a set of written requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: System boundaries are defined too soon or inadequately.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Participants lack the motivation to attend elicitation meetings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Requirements are obvious but go unstated by the customer.

questions.explanation: Volatility problems involve changing requirements or stakeholders being unwilling to finalize or commit to a specific set of written requirements.

questions.text: Which of the following is a primary reason for software project failure related to the interaction between developers and clients?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lack of clear requirements and miscommunication between developer and client.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure to use the latest hardware during the development phase.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Choosing a programming language that is too popular.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hiring too many developers at the start of the project.

questions.explanation: Miscommunication and unclear requirements are top reasons why software projects fail during or after the requirements phase.

questions.text: Business rules are a source of software ______ requirements.
questions.explanation: Business rules often dictate both functional requirements (e.g., how a specific calculation must be performed) and non-functional requirements/constraints (e.g., compliance with legal or organizational policies).

questions.text: According to NIST, ______ of software defects are introduced in the specification phase.
questions.explanation: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and various other studies indicate that approximately 70% of defects are introduced during the early phases of development, specifically during requirements specification.

questions.text: ______ provide constraints on how the system should be designed and built.
questions.explanation: Process requirements (also known as organizational or implementation requirements) specify constraints on the development process, such as the programming language to be used, the development methodology, or design standards.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly lists core activities typically included in the requirements engineering process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation, Management, and Negotiation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coding, Debugging, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance, and Retirement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hardware setup, OS installation, Driver updates, Networking, Backups, and Security

choices.label: D
choices.text: Budgeting, Staffing, Marketing, Sales, Customer Support, and Legal compliance

questions.explanation: The requirements engineering process generally involves inception/elicitation, analysis, elaboration/negotiation, specification, validation, and requirements management.

questions.text: What are the three common categories of problems a software engineer faces during the elicitation of software requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Syntax errors, Runtime errors, and Logic errors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Problems of scope, Problems of understanding, and Problems of volatility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hardware failure, Power outage, and Network latency

choices.label: D
choices.text: High costs, Lack of personnel, and Time constraints

questions.explanation: Software engineering literature (e.g., Pressman) identifies three main problem areas in elicitation: Scope (system boundary is ill-defined), Understanding (users don't know what they want or don't understand technical limits), and Volatility (requirements change over time).

questions.text: Which statement best describes the difference between user requirements and system requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: User requirements are high-level natural language statements for customers; system requirements are detailed technical descriptions for developers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User requirements are written by programmers; system requirements are written by the CEO.

choices.label: C
choices.text: User requirements focus only on hardware; system requirements focus only on software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: User requirements are temporary; system requirements are permanent.

questions.explanation: User requirements are high-level statements in natural language with diagrams that describe what services the system is expected to provide. System requirements are detailed descriptions of the software system's functions, services, and operational constraints.

questions.text: Based on the interaction shown in the scenario (comic), what are two major problems illustrated during the requirements gathering phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The developer is using the wrong programming language and the user wants a discount.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The project lacks a proper budget and the deadline is unrealistic.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Users often do not know what they want or what is possible, and there is a communication gap between users and developers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The computer hardware is obsolete and the software is too slow.

questions.explanation: The comic illustrates a classic deadlock in requirements: the user doesn't know what the software can do, while the developer needs the user to define requirements before starting. It highlights that users often have trouble articulating needs or understanding technical feasibility.

questions.text: Which requirements engineering activity involves checking the requirements document for consistency and completeness?
questions.explanation: **Requirements validation** is the process where the requirements document is checked for consistency and completeness to ensure it meets the stakeholders' needs before further development.

questions.text: In the context of the brainstorming technique for requirements elicitation, what are the two main phases involved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Storm (generating many ideas) and The Calm (filtering ideas)

questions.explanation: Brainstorming typically involves two activities: **The Storm**, which focuses on generating as many ideas as possible (quantity over quality), and **The Calm**, which focuses on filtering, clarifying, and prioritizing those ideas.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of using scenarios during the requirements elicitation process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To define the database schema and table relationships

choices.label: B
choices.text: To provide real-world examples of system characteristics and user responses

choices.label: C
choices.text: To replace the need for stakeholder interviews entirely

choices.label: D
choices.text: To perform final acceptance testing before deployment

questions.explanation: Scenarios are used to describe how a system interacts with users or other systems by presenting specific, real-world examples of characteristics and expected responses.

questions.text: Which of the following best distinguishes a 'Focus group and workshop' from a standard 'Interview' in requirements gathering?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Focus groups are strictly one-on-one sessions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focus groups involve a collaborative session with multiple stakeholders

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interviews are only used for technical troubleshooting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Workshops do not involve any interaction with the customer

questions.explanation: While interviews are typically individual sessions, **Focus groups and workshops** are group-based techniques designed to facilitate discussion and discovery of requirements among several stakeholders simultaneously.

questions.text: In requirements elicitation, what is meant by 'Problems of volatility'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The development team lacks the technical skills to build the system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stakeholders will not commit to a set of written requirements

choices.label: C
choices.text: The customer provides too much detailed information too early

choices.label: D
choices.text: The software is prone to crashing during the elicitation phase

questions.explanation: **Problems of volatility** refer to challenges where requirements change frequently or stakeholders are unwilling to commit to a fixed set of written requirements.
